content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Two years ago, I barely ran a mile. Last May, I crossed the finish line to complete my first marathon. Running went from a workout that felt like a chore to a habit I became excited to keep up. As I have gotten into running more races, I wanted to build a tool that predicts my race time based on running data. Enter RaceTimePredictor.
RaceTimePredictor uses a blend of traditional formulas Riegel and VDOT along with elevation adjustments, to produce a baseline prediction. A gradient boosting model then adds a correction on top of the baseline. The model predicts a time for four standard distances (5K, 10K, Half marathon, Marathon) based on your trailing Strava running activities.
To do this, I first exported my Strava activity data in the desktop browser. I fetched RunSignup finisher data across a variety of races (roughly 7000 finishers). I then trained the prediction model on those results and leveraged it to predict my own race times based from my trailing Strava data.
RaceTimePredictor has four main technical components:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The clients includes the command-line interface (CLI) and Browser with a Next.js graphical user interface (GUI). Both of these clients call the Python FastAPI backend which runs on a separate host. This backend then pulls the activity data and calls the model based on the inputs that the client provided.
CLI
The command-line has three core functionalities:

• sync-corpus - Collect finisher results from curated RunSignup races

• train - Train the hybrid model from a population corpus or Strava export

• predict - Predict race times for 5K, 10K, Half, and Marathon
Input:
race-predictor predict --elev-gain-ft 200 --elev-loss-ft 200 --temp-f 55Output:
Predictions can only be made up until 2026-08-22.Predictions as of 2026-05-30 (+200/-200 ft, 55.0°F)
Distance Predicted Pace
----------------------------------
5K 31:41 10:12/mi
10K 1:06:32 10:42/mi
Half 2:24:19 11:00/mi
Marathon 4:57:35 11:21/mi
Predictions have required fields: elevation gain, elevation loss, and temperature. By default, times are predicted as of your last Strava activity. You can also specify a future date to project how your fitness will carry forward, specifically for an upcoming race.
GUI
Setup for the GUI first includes setting up the API backend:
uvicorn api.main:app --reload --port 8000In a separate terminal window, you will then start the frontend browser:
cd web && npm install && npm run devPress enter or click to view image in full size

RaceTimePredictor is currently in a MVP (minimum viable product) state. Some future improvements that could be made include:

• Allowing users to integrate the application with Strava to pull live activity data per prediction instead of static extraction of data

• Integrating with RunSignup to get a list of upcoming races and allow users to pre-select races which loads elevation and can track weather information based on location

• Using more finisher data to have a more diverse set of training data
